ai.0: In Unit 2 motion ran along a single line, and Newton's laws kept it honest. Now the line breaks open: a thrown ball arcs through the air, a car leans into a bend, a satellite curves forever around the Earth — motion in two dimensions at last. Yet not one of Newton's laws is repealed; they still govern every instant. And beneath the arcs and circles two unbreakable accounts — momentum and energy — balance through every collision, bounce and near-miss. Read it once, in one sitting. Nothing here needs solving, only seeing.
ai.1: Throw a ball across a room and watch its path: not a straight line, but a smooth arch — a parabola. In Unit 2 you could always point along the direction of motion and call it "forwards". A projectile takes that luxury away. It climbs and it travels at once, and to understand it you have to watch two things happening at the same time.
ai.2: Here is the secret that makes the whole of projectile motion simple, and it is worth holding onto: the two motions do not talk to each other. The sideways motion and the up-and-down motion are independent. Sideways, nothing pushes the ball along once it has left your hand — so it covers equal horizontal distances in equal times, drifting on at a steady speed. Up and down, gravity is the only force acting, pulling the ball toward the ground with the same constant acceleration g the whole way.
ai.3: That last point is where almost everyone slips, so plant it now: on the way up, the force on the ball is still downward. Gravity does not pause at the top, switch off, or reverse. A ball thrown straight up is decelerating because the downward pull is fighting its upward motion — but the pull itself never changes. All the way up, all the way down, and at the very peak where the ball is briefly motionless, the acceleration is the same constant g, pointing at the centre of the Earth. There is no such thing as a moment with no force; the only force a projectile ever feels (ignoring the air) is its own weight.
ai.4: Key idea Treat the horizontal and vertical motions separately. Horizontally the ball coasts at constant velocity; vertically it accelerates downward at a constant g the entire flight — rising, falling and at the peak alike. The two stories share only one thing: the clock.
ai.5: The most beautiful demonstration of this independence costs nothing. Hold two identical balls at the same height. Drop one straight down; at the same instant, flick the other horizontally off the table so it sails out across the room. They part company sideways — one lands at your feet, one lands metres away — but listen for the two thuds. They land at the same moment. The sideways shove the second ball got did nothing to its falling; gravity worked on both verticals identically and brought them down together. A bullet fired level across a field and a bullet simply dropped from the same height reach the ground at the same time.
ai.6: Real air spoils the clean parabola a little, and it is worth knowing how. Air resistance opposes the motion — it always points backward along the direction the ball is travelling. So it shaves energy off the flight: the ball does not climb as high, and it does not travel as far. The path becomes lopsided, falling more steeply than it rose. The physics of the ideal parabola still runs underneath; air resistance is the correction the real world adds on top, and for now it is enough to know which way it pushes and what it costs.
ai.7: Trap "At the top of its arc the ball has no force on it because it has stopped." Wrong twice over: a projectile at its peak has stopped moving upward but is still sailing sideways, and gravity is pulling on it as hard as ever. Velocity can be zero for an instant; the force never is.
ai.8: Connects The full projectile toolkit — resolving the launch into components, the symmetry of the flight, range and time of flight, and a proper qualitative account of air resistance — is built in Pass 2 · Projectile motion. The component-resolution you will lean on was banked back in Unit 2's work on vectors.
ai.9: Now bend the straight line right around into a circle. Whirl a ball on a string above your head, drive a car around a roundabout, ride a fairground rotor — in each case something travels a circular path at steady speed. This is uniform circular motion, and it hides a subtlety that has fooled people for centuries.
ai.10: Moving at constant speed sounds like nothing is happening — no speeding up, no slowing down. But velocity is not just speed; it is speed with a direction, and on a circle the direction is changing every instant. The ball is always heading straight ahead along the curve — its velocity points along the tangent, the way it would fly off if the string snapped. To keep bending that velocity around the circle instead of letting it run straight, something must pull the object constantly toward the centre. The object is accelerating — not because it is getting faster, but because it is forever turning.
ai.11: That inward pull has a name, the centripetal ("centre-seeking") force, and a single relationship governs it: Fnet = mv²r. Go faster, or turn a tighter circle, and the inward force needed grows. The acceleration points straight at the centre; the velocity points along the tangent, at right angles to it. They are always perpendicular.
ai.12: Key idea In uniform circular motion the velocity is tangential and the net force and acceleration are radial — pointing to the centre. The centripetal force is not a new kind of force; it is the job done by some real, ordinary force that happens to point inward.
ai.13: Which brings us to the great illusion. Sit in a car that swings hard around a bend and you feel flung against the door — thrown outward, surely, by some "centrifugal force". There is no such force. Nothing is pushing you outward. What is really happening is that your body, obeying Newton's first law, wants to keep going straight ahead — and it is the door, curving inward across your path, that shoves you toward the centre and drags you around the bend with the car. The pressure you feel against the door is the door pushing inward on you; the "flung outward" sensation is just your inertia trying to escape the turn. Every real force in a circle points inward.
ai.14: So in any circular motion, the honest question is always: which real force is doing the pulling? Whirl a ball on a string and the string's tension hauls it inward. Drive a car around a flat bend and it is friction between the tyres and the road — lose that friction on ice and the car slides straight on, exactly as the first law promises. Build a banked track, tilting the road inward like a velodrome or a freeway off-ramp, and the road's own push — the normal force, angled inward by the tilt — can supply the turn. At the right "design" speed a banked track needs no friction at all: the sideways lean of the normal force alone bends the car around, while its upward part carries the car's weight. That is why race tracks and high-speed curves are banked.
ai.15: Circles can stand up out of the ground, too. Swing a bucket of water in a vertical loop, or take a car over a humped bridge and down into a dip, and gravity now helps or fights the turn depending on where you are. At the very top of a vertical loop both the support force and gravity point downward — both toward the centre — so they add together to bend the path. There is a slowest speed you can manage up there: the speed at which gravity alone supplies exactly the inward pull the circle needs, the support force having dropped to nothing. Go any slower and gravity is more than the circle calls for, so it curves the object inward off the path and it falls away. At the bottom of the loop the support must overcome gravity and supply the inward turn, so it is largest there — which is exactly why you feel heaviest at the bottom of a dip and lightest, even floating, over a crest.
ai.16: Trap There is no outward "centrifugal force". The net force in circular motion always points to the centre, and it is supplied by a real, identifiable force — tension, friction, a normal force, or gravity. Name that force, and the problem is half solved. Blame an imaginary outward push, and you will get the direction of everything wrong.
ai.17: Lift the circle off the planet entirely and you have the grandest example of all: a satellite. A satellite — the Moon, or the spacecraft that carry your phone's location and the weather maps — is simply an object falling toward the Earth and missing, over and over, forever. Gravity is the inward force, the string you cannot see, and it bends the satellite's straight-ahead motion into a closed loop. Here we treat that loop purely as circular motion: an object curving around at steady speed because a centre-pointing force holds it there. Why that force is gravity, how its strength sets the orbit, and why a higher orbit means a slower satellite — that is the gravitation story, and it belongs to the Fields chapter, where the field doing the pulling is mapped properly.
ai.18: Connects Horizontal circles — the string, the flat road and the banked track — and vertical circles at their top and bottom are built in Pass 2 · Circular motion. The satellite as orbiting object, and the gravity that holds it, is developed in the Fields story · Gravity and orbits (Unit 3 AOS 2) — same circular motion, seen from the force that supplies it.
ai.19: Watch two cars collide, two pool balls click apart, a spacecraft fire its thrusters. The motion looks chaotic — but underneath, the universe keeps two ledgers that never fail to balance. They are momentum and energy, and learning to read them is learning to predict what comes out of a collision without watching the collision itself.
ai.20: The first ledger is momentum, the quantity p = mv — how much motion an object carries, mass times velocity. The crucial word is velocity: momentum is a vector, it has a direction. In an isolated system — one where outside forces do not interfere — the total momentum before any interaction exactly equals the total momentum after. Two skaters push apart and drift in opposite directions; a rifle kicks back as the bullet flies forward; a rocket throws gas one way and climbs the other. In every case, add up the momentum with its directions kept straight — one way counted positive, the other negative — and the books balance perfectly. Momentum is conserved in every collision, no exceptions.
ai.21: Key idea Momentum is a vector. Pick a positive direction, keep the signs consistent, and the total momentum of an isolated system is the same before and after any collision — always, in every kind of collision.
ai.22: Closely related is impulse: a force acting over a time, FΔt, which is exactly the change in an object's momentum, mΔv. This is why a long, soft stop is gentler than a short, hard one. A car's crumple zone, an airbag, a cricketer's hands giving with the ball, a high-jumper landing on a thick mat — each stretches the stopping time, and for the same change in momentum a longer time means a smaller force. The same idea explains the punch of a struck ball: a big force over a brief contact delivers a big change in momentum.
ai.23: The second ledger is energy, and it comes in several currencies that trade into one another. There is kinetic energy, Ek = ½mv², the energy of motion. There is gravitational potential energy, Eg = mgΔh, the energy stored by lifting something up, ready to be cashed back as the object falls. And there is elastic (spring) potential energy, Es = ½kx², stored in anything stretched or compressed — a bow, a trampoline, a bumper. On a roller-coaster these trade back and forth all day: height converts to speed down the first drop, speed converts back to height up the next rise, and a launch spring dumps its stored energy into motion at the start.
ai.24: Notice the shape of that spring formula — energy depends on x², the square of how far you stretch it. Pull a spring twice as far and you store not twice but four times the energy. That steep, quadratic climb is why a slightly longer draw on a bow sends the arrow so much faster, and why a small extra compression of a bumper matters so much in a crash.
ai.25: Here the two ledgers part ways, and the difference is the whole point. Momentum is always conserved in a collision. Kinetic energy is not. When two objects bounce apart cleanly — billiard balls, or two superballs — almost no kinetic energy is lost; physicists call this an elastic collision, the only kind where the kinetic-energy books also balance. But when two cars crumple together and move off as one wreck, or a ball of putty splats and sticks, kinetic energy goes missing. That is an inelastic collision.
ai.26: Missing — but not destroyed. Energy is never destroyed; it only changes form. The kinetic energy that vanishes from a crash has been spent bending metal, in the heat of the crumpling, and in the bang you hear. Add up the heat, the sound and the energy of permanent deformation, and the books balance after all. So the practised eye reads a collision like an accountant: momentum, always equal before and after; kinetic energy, equal only if the collision was elastic — and if it fell, the shortfall is precisely the heat, sound and crumpling carried away.
ai.27: Trap "Energy was lost in the crash, so conservation failed." Energy is never lost — it is converted. In an inelastic collision the missing kinetic energy reappears as heat, sound and deformation; the total energy is conserved. And momentum is conserved either way. Do not confuse "kinetic energy not conserved" with "energy disappeared".
ai.28: Connects Momentum conservation, impulse from a force–time graph, elastic versus inelastic collisions, and the full energy ledger — including work as the area under a force–distance graph and the quadratic spring store ½kx² — are worked through in Pass 2 · Momentum and energy. The work, energy and impulse ideas were first banked in Unit 2; here they meet two-dimensional collisions.
ai.29: You have now seen the whole Area of Study once: a straight line broken open into arcs and circles, with Newton's laws unbroken beneath every curve, and two ledgers — momentum and energy — that the universe never lets slip. A projectile that feels only gravity and falls in two independent motions at once; a circle held together by a real inward force and never an imaginary outward one; satellites falling around the world forever; and collisions read off two balancing accounts. Everything from here is the same story at higher resolution.
ai.30: Pass 2 carries it in three chapters, each meant for the night before that topic meets you in class: projectile motion, with components and air resistance; circular motion, horizontal and banked and vertical; and momentum and energy, with collisions and the full energy ledger. There you will find every examinable equation with its formula-sheet status, the standard graphs, and quick checks to confirm each idea has landed.
ai.31: Pass 3 is for exam season: the key-knowledge dot points in study-design order, with the classic traps spelled out, the assessors' advice, and exam-style questions with full marking solutions. The depth row below carries you down whenever you are ready, and this page stays one click away for whenever the detail starts to obscure the story.

socratic.0:
Socrates. In your earlier studies, motion ran along a single straight line, obedient to Newton's laws. Tell me, does a thrown ball travel in a straight line?
Student. No, it arcs through the air. And a car turning a bend curves, and a satellite circles the Earth without ever stopping.
Socrates. Just so. We are leaving the single line behind for motion in two dimensions. Now, do you suppose Newton's laws are repealed once the path bends?
Student. I doubt it. They must still govern every instant, even beneath the arcs and circles.
Socrates. And if I told you that two quantities, momentum and energy, stay balanced through every collision and interaction, would you trust that?
Student. I would like to see why. Let us walk through it once, slowly, without solving anything yet.
Socrates. Then let us read this through in one sitting, and reason rather than calculate.
socratic.1:
Socrates. Picture a ball thrown across a room. What shape does its path take?
Student. Not a straight line, but a smooth arch, a parabola.
Socrates. In the old one-dimensional world, you could simply call one direction "forwards". Can you do that here?
Student. No, because the ball rises and travels sideways at the same time. There is no single direction that captures it.
Socrates. So how many motions must we consider at once?
Student. Two, occurring together. I cannot understand the arc by watching only one of them.
socratic.2:
Socrates. Here is the key that unlocks it: those two motions do not depend on one another. They are independent. Once the ball leaves your hand, what acts on it horizontally?
Student. Nothing, neglecting air. So horizontally it should just keep going.
Socrates. Meaning it covers what kind of horizontal distances in equal times?
Student. Equal ones, at constant speed.
Socrates. And vertically, what acts?
Student. Only gravity, giving the same constant acceleration g toward the ground for the whole flight.
socratic.3:
Socrates. Now a question that trips many people. On the way up, which way does the force on the ball point?
Student. Downward, surely. Gravity does not pause at the top, or switch off, or reverse.
Socrates. Then why does a ball thrown straight up slow down?
Student. Because the downward force opposes its upward motion. The motion is opposed, but the force itself never changes.
Socrates. So during the ascent, the descent, and the instant at the peak when the ball is motionless, what is the acceleration?
Student. The same constant g, aimed at the centre of the Earth. There is no instant when no force acts. Neglecting air resistance, the only force on a projectile is its own weight.
socratic.4:
Key idea Socrates. Say back to me, then, how we treat the two motions.
Student. Separately. Horizontally the ball moves at constant velocity; vertically it accelerates downward at a constant g through the whole flight, rising, falling, and at the peak alike.
Socrates. And what links the two?
Student. Only the elapsed time they share.
socratic.5:
Socrates. Let us test this independence. Hold two identical balls at the same height. Drop one straight down; at the same instant fire the other horizontally off the table. Which lands first?
Student. I would once have guessed the dropped one. But if the motions are independent, they should land at the same moment.
Socrates. And they do. Why does the sideways velocity not delay the fall?
Student. Because horizontal velocity has no effect on the vertical fall. Gravity acts identically on both, and brings them down together.
Socrates. So a bullet fired horizontally across a field and a bullet simply dropped from the same height?
Student. Reach the ground at the same time. Remarkable, but it follows.
socratic.6:
Socrates. Our parabola has been ideal. What does real air do to it?
Student. Air resistance opposes the motion, always acting backward along the direction of travel.
Socrates. And its effect on the flight?
Student. It removes energy, so the ball rises less high and travels less far. The path turns asymmetrical, falling more steeply than it rose.
Socrates. Does this overturn the parabola?
Student. No. The ideal parabolic motion is still the underlying behaviour; air resistance is just a correction from real conditions. For now it is enough to know its direction and its effect.
socratic.7:
Trap Socrates. Someone insists: "At the top of its arc the ball has no force on it because it has stopped." Is that right?
Student. It is wrong twice over. At the peak the ball has only stopped moving upward; it is still moving horizontally. And gravity acts on it unchanged. The velocity can be zero for an instant, but the force is never zero.
socratic.8:
Connects Socrates. Where might you go to see this worked out fully, with launch resolved into components, the symmetry of the flight, range, time of flight, and a careful account of air resistance?
Student. In Pass 2 · Projectile motion. And the component resolution there rests on the vector work I met in Unit 2.
socratic.9:
Socrates. Now let us bend the straight line into a circle. A ball whirled on a string overhead, a car on a roundabout, a fairground rotor, what do they share?
Student. Each is an object travelling a circular path at constant speed. Uniform circular motion.
Socrates. Simple enough, you might think. But it hides a subtlety that has confused people for centuries. Shall we draw it out?
Student. Please.
socratic.10:
Socrates. If the speed is constant, is anything changing?
Student. At first it seems not, no speeding up, no slowing down.
Socrates. But tell me, is velocity the same thing as speed?
Student. No, velocity is speed with a direction. And on a circle the direction changes at every instant.
Socrates. In which direction does the object actually move at any moment?
Student. Along the tangent, the way it would fly off if the string were cut.
Socrates. So to keep bending that velocity around the circle instead of letting it run straight, what is needed?
Student. A force acting continuously toward the centre. The object is accelerating, not because its speed grows, but because its direction is forever changing.
socratic.11:
Socrates. This inward force has a name, "centre-seeking". What is it called?
Student. The centripetal force. And one relationship governs it: Fnet = mv²r.
Socrates. So a greater speed, or a tighter circle?
Student. Demands a greater inward force. The acceleration points to the centre, the velocity runs along the tangent, and the two are always perpendicular.
socratic.12:
Key idea Socrates. Summarise the geometry for me.
Student. In uniform circular motion the velocity is tangential, while the net force and acceleration are radial, directed toward the centre.
Socrates. And is the centripetal force some new kind of force?
Student. No, it is the role played by some real, ordinary force that happens to point inward.
socratic.13:
Socrates. Now, a person in a car taking a sharp bend feels pressed against the door, as if flung outward by a "centrifugal force". Does such a force exist?
Student. No. Nothing pushes the person outward.
Socrates. Then what is really happening?
Student. By Newton's first law the body tends to continue in a straight line. The door, curving inward across that path, pushes the body toward the centre and carries it around with the car.
Socrates. So the pressure felt against the door is?
Student. The door pushing inward on the body. The feeling of being thrown outward is just the body's inertia trying to go straight. Every real force in circular motion points inward.
socratic.14:
Socrates. Then the whole task in any circular motion is to ask one question. What is it?
Student. Which real force provides the inward pull?
Socrates. For a ball on a string?
Student. The string's tension, acting inward.
Socrates. For a car on a flat bend?
Student. Friction between tyres and road. Lose it on ice and the car runs straight ahead, exactly as the first law predicts.
Socrates. And on a banked track, tilted inward like a velodrome or a freeway off-ramp?
Student. The road's normal force, angled inward by the tilt, can supply the turn. At the right "design" speed no friction is needed at all: the inward component of the normal force bends the car around, while its upward component holds up its weight. That is why race tracks and high-speed curves are banked.
socratic.15:
Socrates. Circles need not lie flat. Swing a bucket of water in a vertical loop, or drive over a humped bridge and through a dip. How does gravity behave there?
Student. It assists or opposes the turn depending on position.
Socrates. At the very top of a vertical loop, which way do the support force and gravity point?
Student. Both downward, both toward the centre, so they add together to bend the path.
Socrates. Is there a slowest speed you can manage at the top?
Student. Yes, a minimum: the speed where gravity alone supplies exactly the inward force needed, with the support force fallen to zero. Go slower and gravity exceeds what is needed, so the object curves inward off the path and falls away.
Socrates. And at the bottom of the loop?
Student. There the support force must beat gravity and still supply the inward force, so it is greatest. That is why you feel heaviest at the bottom of a dip and lightest, even weightless, at the top of a crest.
socratic.16:
Trap Socrates. So if a problem tempts you to invoke an outward "centrifugal force"?
Student. I refuse it, for there is none. The net force in circular motion always points to the centre, supplied by a real, identifiable force: tension, friction, a normal force, or gravity.
Socrates. And the cost of that imaginary outward push?
Student. It reverses my directions throughout the problem. Identifying the true inward force is most of the analysis.
socratic.17:
Socrates. This reasoning need not stop at the ground. Consider a satellite, the Moon, or a spacecraft sending weather images. What is it doing?
Student. Continuously falling toward the Earth and continuously missing.
Socrates. What plays the part of the inward force?
Student. Gravity, an unseen constraint that bends the satellite's straight-ahead motion into a closed loop.
Socrates. And here we treat that loop simply as?
Student. Circular motion: an object curving at constant speed because a centre-directed force holds it on the path. Why that force is gravity, how its size sets the orbit, and why a higher orbit means a slower satellite, all belong to the gravitation treatment in the Fields chapter, where the field that supplies the force is set out in full.
socratic.18:
Connects Socrates. Where are the horizontal circles, the string, the flat road, the banked track, and the vertical circles at top and bottom, developed in full?
Student. In Pass 2 · Circular motion. And the satellite as an orbiting object, with the gravity that holds it, is developed in the Fields story · Gravity and orbits (Unit 3 AOS 2), the same circular motion seen from the force that supplies it.
socratic.19:
Socrates. Now to collisions. Two cars colliding, two pool balls separating, a spacecraft firing its thrusters, do these not look like pure disorder?
Student. They do, at a glance.
Socrates. Yet two quantities run unbroken beneath them. Which?
Student. Momentum and energy, always conserved.
Socrates. And what power does that give us?
Student. We can predict the outcome of a collision without ever analysing the messy collision itself.
socratic.20:
Socrates. Take the first conserved quantity, momentum. How is it defined?
Student. As p = mv, the product of mass and velocity.
Socrates. Note that word, velocity. What does it tell us about momentum?
Student. That it is a vector and carries a direction.
Socrates. In an isolated system, with no external forces, what holds between the total momentum before and after an interaction?
Student. They are equal. Two skaters push apart in opposite directions; a rifle recoils as the bullet flies forward; a rocket throws gas one way and accelerates the other.
Socrates. And when you add the momenta with directions kept straight?
Student. Taking one direction positive and the other negative, the total is unchanged. Momentum is conserved in every collision, without exception.
socratic.21:
Key idea Socrates. State the rule cleanly.
Student. Momentum is a vector. With a chosen positive direction and consistent signs, the total momentum of an isolated system is the same before and after any collision, in every kind of collision.
socratic.22:
Socrates. Closely tied to momentum is impulse. What is it?
Student. A force acting over a time, FΔt, which equals the change in an object's momentum, mΔv.
Socrates. So if you must absorb the same change in momentum, is it gentler to stop quickly or slowly?
Student. Slowly. A longer, gentler stop produces a smaller force than a short, sudden one.
Socrates. Give me the everyday cases.
Student. A car's crumple zone, an airbag, a fielder's hands moving with the ball, a high jumper landing on a thick mat, each stretches the stopping time, and for the same change in momentum a longer time means a smaller force. The same relationship, run the other way, explains a struck ball: a large force over a brief contact delivers a large change in momentum.
socratic.23:
Socrates. The second conserved quantity is energy, which wears several forms that convert into one another. Name them.
Student. Kinetic energy, Ek = ½mv², the energy of motion. Gravitational potential energy, Eg = mgΔh, stored by raising an object and returned as it falls. And elastic (spring) potential energy, Es = ½kx², stored in anything stretched or compressed, like a bow, a trampoline, or a bumper.
Socrates. Where might you watch all three trade places?
Student. On a roller-coaster: height becomes speed on a drop, speed becomes height again on a rise, and a launch spring pours its stored energy into motion at the start.
socratic.24:
Socrates. Look closely at the spring form. On what does it depend?
Student. On x², the square of the extension.
Socrates. So if you stretch a spring twice as far, you store twice the energy?
Student. No, four times the energy, because of the squaring.
Socrates. And what does that quadratic dependence explain?
Student. Why a slightly longer draw on a bow gives the arrow much greater speed, and why a small extra compression of a bumper has a large effect in a collision.
socratic.25:
Socrates. Here the two conserved quantities part ways, and the difference is everything. Is momentum conserved in a collision?
Student. Always.
Socrates. And kinetic energy?
Student. Not always.
Socrates. When two objects rebound cleanly, billiard balls, or two superballs, what happens to the kinetic energy?
Student. Almost none is lost. That is an elastic collision, the only kind in which kinetic energy is also conserved.
Socrates. And when two cars crumple together and move off as one mass, or putty deforms and sticks?
Student. Kinetic energy is lost. That is an inelastic collision.
socratic.26:
Socrates. You say kinetic energy is lost. Is it destroyed?
Student. No, energy is never destroyed; it only changes form.
Socrates. Then where does the missing kinetic energy go?
Student. Into deforming the material, into heat in the crumpling, and into sound. Count those in, and the total energy is conserved.
Socrates. So how do we analyse any collision?
Student. Momentum is always equal before and after; kinetic energy is equal only if the collision was elastic; and any shortfall in kinetic energy equals the heat, sound and deformation produced.
socratic.27:
Trap Socrates. Someone declares: "Energy was lost in the crash, so conservation failed." Do you accept it?
Student. No. Energy is never lost, only converted. In an inelastic collision the missing kinetic energy reappears as heat, sound and deformation, and the total energy is conserved. Momentum is conserved either way. "Kinetic energy not conserved" must never be confused with "energy disappeared".
socratic.28:
Connects Socrates. And where is all this developed in full, the momentum conservation, impulse from a force–time graph, elastic versus inelastic collisions, and the whole energy treatment?
Student. In Pass 2 · Momentum and energy, with work as the area under a force–distance graph and the quadratic spring store ½kx². The work, energy and impulse ideas came from Unit 2; here they are applied to two-dimensional collisions.
socratic.29:
Socrates. We have now surveyed the whole Area of Study once. Can you gather it in your own words?
Student. A straight line was extended into arcs and circles, with Newton's laws holding beneath every curve, and two conserved quantities, momentum and energy, maintained throughout.
Socrates. And the four pillars?
Student. A projectile feels only gravity and falls in two independent motions at once; a circle is held by a real inward force and never an imaginary outward one; satellites fall around the Earth continuously; and collisions are read through two conserved quantities.
Socrates. Then we have the structure. What remains?
Student. The rest of the material develops this same content at greater resolution.
socratic.30:
Socrates. How does Pass 2 lay this out?
Student. In three chapters, each meant to be read before the topic is taught in class: projectile motion, with components and air resistance; circular motion, horizontal, banked and vertical; and momentum and energy, with collisions and the full energy treatment.
Socrates. And what does each chapter carry?
Student. Every examinable equation with its formula-sheet status, the standard graphs, and checks to confirm each idea has been understood.
socratic.31:
Socrates. And Pass 3, what is it for?
Student. Exam preparation: the key-knowledge dot points in study-design order, the common traps named, the assessors' advice, and exam-style questions with full marking solutions.
Socrates. And if the detail ever obscures the whole?
Student. The depth row below supplies it when required, and this page stays one click away whenever I need to see the overall structure again.
